prs.body:
- [x] I have read and followed the contribution guidelines.

- [x] I have read and followed the how to open a pull request guide.

- [x] My pull request targets the `main` branch of freeCodeCamp.

- [x] I have tested these changes either locally on my machine, or GitHub Codespaces.

Closes #67300

## Description

Updated the DOM lesson wording in `what-is-the-dom-and-how-do-you-access-elements` to clarify that not all DOM nodes are HTML elements.

The sentence now explains that many DOM nodes are elements, while others can be text nodes, comments, or the document itself. This better reflects actual DOM behavior and helps prevent learner confusion.

prs.title: MAINT: Add automated AI-slop detection workflow and document policy
prs.body:
<!--         ----------------------------------------------------------------
                MAKE SURE YOUR PR GETS THE ATTENTION IT DESERVES!
                ----------------------------------------------------------------

*  FORMAT IT RIGHT:
      https://www.numpy.org/devdocs/dev/development_workflow.html#writing-the-commit-message

*  IF IT'S A NEW FEATURE OR API CHANGE, TEST THE WATERS:
      https://www.numpy.org/devdocs/dev/development_workflow.html#get-the-mailing-list-s-opinion

*  HIT ALL THE GUIDELINES:
      https://numpy.org/devdocs/dev/index.html#guidelines

*  WHAT TO DO IF WE HAVEN'T GOTTEN BACK TO YOU:
      https://www.numpy.org/devdocs/dev/development_workflow.html#getting-your-pr-reviewed
-->

Description

Fixes #30962

This PR introduces an automated workflow to assist maintainers in identifying potentially low-quality or AI-generated pull requests. The workflow adds a label to flagged PRs for manual review rather than automatically closing them. Documentation is also updated to make contributors aware that such automated checks exist.

The goal is to provide maintainers with an early signal for PRs that may require additional review while avoiding unnecessary friction for legitimate contributors.

Changes
GitHub Actions workflow

.github/workflows/anti-slop.yml

Added a new workflow that runs the peakoss/anti-slop action.

Key configuration details:

Trigger:
Runs on pull_request_target when a pull request is opened, reopened, or edited.

Behavior:
The workflow does not automatically close PRs (close-pr: false). Instead, it adds a label (AI slop?) so maintainers can review the content manually.

Detection patterns:
Includes blocked terms and phrases identified in the issue discussion, such as:

streamline

facilitate

comprehensive

A key takeaway is

This underscores the importance of

Exemptions:
PRs created by repository Owners, Members, and Collaborators are excluded to reduce false positives for trusted contributors.

Feedback to contributors:
When a PR is flagged, the workflow posts an informational comment explaining that the PR was flagged for manual review and links to the detection tool.

Documentation updates

doc/source/dev/ai_policy.rst

Added a new Automated Checks section to the AI policy documentation.

The section explains:

that automated tools may be used to detect low-quality or AI-generated content

that flagged PRs are labeled for review rather than automatically rejected

that the goal is to assist maintainers while maintaining a welcoming contribution environment

Motivation

Large open-source projects receive many pull requests, and maintainers must efficiently identify submissions that may require additional scrutiny. This workflow provides a lightweight signal for potential AI-generated or low-effort content while ensuring that legitimate contributors are not discouraged by automatic rejection.

By labeling rather than closing flagged PRs and documenting the process transparently, this change aims to improve review efficiency while maintaining a contributor-friendly workflow.

Verification

Confirmed the workflow triggers on PR open/edit/reopen events.

Verified that flagged PRs receive the AI slop? label.

Confirmed the workflow posts an explanatory comment when a PR is flagged.

Verified that PRs from repository Owners/Members/Collaborators are not flagged.

Confirmed documentation builds successfully with the new section in ai_policy.rst.

prs.title: MAINT: Deprecate PyArray_BufferConverter and remove Python 2 buffer protocol remnants
prs.body:
…l remnants

<!--         ----------------------------------------------------------------
                MAKE SURE YOUR PR GETS THE ATTENTION IT DESERVES!
                ----------------------------------------------------------------

*  FORMAT IT RIGHT:
      https://www.numpy.org/devdocs/dev/development_workflow.html#writing-the-commit-message

*  IF IT'S A NEW FEATURE OR API CHANGE, TEST THE WATERS:
      https://www.numpy.org/devdocs/dev/development_workflow.html#get-the-mailing-list-s-opinion

*  HIT ALL THE GUIDELINES:
      https://numpy.org/devdocs/dev/index.html#guidelines

*  WHAT TO DO IF WE HAVEN'T GOTTEN BACK TO YOU:
      https://www.numpy.org/devdocs/dev/development_workflow.html#getting-your-pr-reviewed
-->
Description
Fixes #30813
This PR removes remaining remnants of the legacy Python 2 buffer protocol from NumPy internals and moves related structures toward the modern Python buffer protocol. The changes isolate legacy structures from the public API, introduce internal definitions where still required for compatibility, and formally deprecate legacy conversion helpers.

These updates reduce technical debt while preserving source and binary compatibility for downstream projects.
Changes
Public header modernization
numpy/_core/include/numpy/ndarraytypes.h
Make PyArray_Chunk opaque when building NumPy internally (NPY_INTERNAL_BUILD).
Prevent internal code from relying on the legacy public definition while maintaining compatibility for external extensions.
Internal compatibility layer
numpy/_core/src/multiarray/common.h
Added a binary-compatible internal definition of:

struct _PyArray_Chunk
Ensures internal code paths such as the ndarray constructor (array_new) continue to work during the transition.
Deprecation of legacy converters
numpy/_core/src/multiarray/conversion_utils.c

Added a deprecation warning to:

PyArray_BufferConverter

Marked as deprecated starting in NumPy 2.5.

Users are encouraged to use the standard Python buffer protocol instead.

Documentation updates

doc/source/reference/c-api/array.rst

Updated documentation for PyArray_FromBuffer.

Removed references to the legacy __buffer__ attribute and the single-segment buffer protocol.

Added:

.. deprecated:: 2.5

for PyArray_BufferConverter.

Verification

Confirmed np.ndarray(..., buffer=...) triggers the expected deprecation warning.

Verified np.frombuffer continues to work normally without warnings.

Ensured existing internal usage of PyArray_Chunk remains functional.

Motivation
These changes remove remaining dependencies on legacy Python 2 buffer protocol mechanisms while preserving compatibility with existing internal code paths. This reduces technical debt and moves NumPy further toward exclusive use of the modern Python buffer protocol.

prs.title: BUG: Use BufferError for unsupported devices in DLPack and Array API
prs.body:
Fixes #30937
The Array API specification requires that functions encountering unsupported devices or incompatible DLPack features raise BufferError. Previously, several code paths in NumPy raised ValueError or RuntimeError instead. This PR standardizes the behavior across both C and Python implementations to ensure compliance with the specification and improve error consistency.
C-level updates
numpy/_core/src/multiarray/dlpack.c

Updated from_dlpack to raise BufferError instead of RuntimeError when encountering unsupported or incompatible DLPack inputs, including:
unsupported device types
unsupported dtype lanes
unsupported DLPack dtypes
tensors exceeding NPY_MAXDIMS
numpy/_core/src/multiarray/conversion_utils.c

Updated PyArray_DeviceConverterOptional to raise BufferError instead of ValueError when an unsupported device string (anything other than "cpu") is provided.
numpy/_core/src/multiarray/array_api_standard.c
Updated array_to_device to raise BufferError when an unsupported device is requested, aligning with the Array API specification for to_device.

Python-level updates
numpy/_core/numeric.py

Updated the Array API compatible astype implementation to raise BufferError instead of ValueError when an unsupported device string is provided.
numpy/_core/_array_api_info.py

Updated default_dtypes and dtypes inspection functions to raise BufferError when an unsupported device argument is passed.

Tests

Updated tests to reflect the new error behavior:
numpy/_core/tests/test_dlpack.py
Updated tests for np.from_dlpack(..., device="gpu") to expect BufferError.

numpy/_core/tests/test_array_api_info.py
Updated inspection function tests to expect BufferError when unsupported devices are provided.

numpy/_core/tests/test_multiarray.py
Updated TestDevice.test_device tests for creator functions (arange, zeros, empty, asarray, etc.) to expect BufferError for invalid device strings.

Added a new test verifying that to_device raises BufferError when an unsupported device is requested.

Motivation
This change ensures that device and buffer compatibility errors consistently raise BufferError, as required by the Array API specification. This improves consistency across NumPy’s DLPack and Array API code paths and aligns implementation behavior with documented expectations.

prs.title: Enhance dagitty interoperability and support for ADMG, PAG, and varia…
prs.body:
# Enhance dagitty interoperability and support for ADMG, PAG, and variable roles (Issue #2352)

## Checklist

- [x] Make sure you are requesting to pull a topic/feature/bugfix branch (right side) against the dev branch (left side). Please do not request the dev branch.
- [x] Followed all the steps from the Contributing Guide (https://github.com/pgmpy/pgmpy/blob/dev/Contributing.md).
- [x] All GitHub Actions checks are passing.

### If a Large Language Model (LLM) was used to assist with changes:

- [x] Included a short paragraph describing the changes made, written manually without AI assistance.
- [x] Manually verified that the changes behave as expected and considered edge cases.
- [x] Ensured no unnecessary try-except blocks were introduced.
- [x] If tests were generated with assistance, verified and consolidated them appropriately, and ensured they cover meaningful scenarios.

## Issue number(s) that this pull request fixes

Fixes #2352

## List of changes to the codebase in this pull request

- Added a new PAG class to support Partial Ancestral Graph representations.
- Extended ADMG, PDAG, DAG, and AncestralBase to improve dagitty compatibility.
- Standardised variable role parsing in parser.py, including consistent handling of exposure, outcome, and related roles.
- Updated and expanded unit tests to cover:
  - Parsing of variable roles from dagitty input.
  - Correct construction and behaviour of ADMG and PAG structures.
  - Interoperability between graph classes.
- Refactored internal logic where necessary to ensure consistent behaviour across graph types.

## Description of the changes

This PR enhances dagitty interoperability within pgmpy by adding support for Partial Ancestral Graphs (PAG) and improving handling of Ancestral Directed Mixed Graphs (ADMG). It standardises variable role parsing to ensure consistent interpretation of exposure, outcome, and related roles when importing models. The implementation introduces a new PAG class, updates existing graph classes (ADMG, PDAG, DAG, and AncestralBase) for improved structural compatibility, and modifies the parser to normalise role definitions. The changes were manually verified to ensure correct graph construction, consistent role handling, and compatibility with existing APIs, including edge case handling for mixed edge types and role assignments.

prs.title: feat(table): add conditional formatting support for categorical columns
prs.body:
Fixes #37678

### SUMMARY
This PR enables conditional formatting for categorical columns (String and Boolean)
in both the Standard Table plugin and the AG Grid Table plugin.

Previously, categorical columns were blocked by control panel filtering logic,
even though the conditional formatting UI already supported categorical operators.

This change removes those restrictions and allows categorical columns to be used
in conditional formatting rules.

Changes made:

Standard Table plugin
File:
superset-frontend/plugins/plugin-chart-table/src/controlPanel.tsx

- Removed the `!hasTimeComparison` restriction that prevented String and Boolean
  columns from appearing in the conditional formatting selector.
- Updated description text from:
  "Apply conditional color formatting to numeric columns"
  to:
  "Apply conditional color formatting to columns".

AG Grid Table plugin
File:
superset-frontend/plugins/plugin-chart-ag-grid-table/src/controlPanel.tsx

- Updated filtering logic to include:
  - GenericDataType.String
  - GenericDataType.Boolean
  in addition to GenericDataType.Numeric.
- Updated description/label text for consistency.

User impact:
- String columns now support operators like:
  equals, begins with, ends with, contains, not contains
- Boolean columns now support operators like:
  is true, is false, is null, is not null

Technical notes:
- Reused existing `GenericDataType` enum.
- Minimal, targeted diff without structural refactoring.
- Code formatted using Prettier.

All relevant tests passed (254 tests).

### BEFORE/AFTER SCREENSHOTS OR ANIMATED GIF
Not applicable.

### TESTING INSTRUCTIONS
1. Run Superset locally.
2. Create or open a dataset containing:
   - String columns
   - Boolean columns
3. Create a Table visualization (Standard Table or AG Grid Table).
4. Open "Conditional Formatting".
5. Verify that:
   - String columns appear in the column selector
   - Boolean columns appear in the column selector
6. Add formatting rules using categorical operators.
7. Confirm formatting applies correctly in the rendered table.

### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
- [x] Has associated issue: Fixes #37678
- [ ] Required feature flags:
- [ ] Changes UI
- [ ] Includes DB Migration (follow approval process in SIP-59)
  - [ ] Migration is atomic, supports rollback & is backwards-compatible
  - [ ] Confirm DB migration upgrade and downgrade tested
  - [ ] Runtime estimates and downtime expectations provided
- [x] Introduces new feature or API
- [ ] Removes existing feature or API

prs.title: fix(oracle): return TIMESTAMP for time grain truncation to prevent invalid date values (#37608)
prs.body:
SUMMARY

This PR fixes the Oracle time-grain behavior described in #37608, where charts (such as Box Plot visualizations) could display incorrect date values (for example, years like 8959) when grouping by time.
The issue occurs because Oracle’s TRUNC() function returns a DATE, while Superset expects a TIMESTAMP for time-series grouping. This mismatch causes incorrect date interpretation downstream.
This PR ensures that all Oracle time-grain expressions return a TIMESTAMP.

Changes made
Oracle engine spec update
File:
superset/db_engine_specs/oracle.py
Time-grain expressions now cast truncated values to TIMESTAMP:

SECOND now returns:
CAST({col} as TIMESTAMP)
Other grains now follow the pattern:

CAST(TRUNC(CAST({col} as DATE), '<grain>') AS TIMESTAMP)
This guarantees consistent timestamp typing across all time grains.

Unit test update
File:
tests/unit_tests/db_engine_specs/test_oracle.py
Updated the monthly-grain test assertion to verify the new SQL output:

Before:
TRUNC(CAST("decimal" as DATE), 'MONTH')
After:
CAST(TRUNC(CAST("decimal" as DATE), 'MONTH') AS TIMESTAMP)
This ensures future changes do not regress Oracle time-grain behavior.

Why this fix works
Oracle:
TRUNC(date) → returns DATE

Superset expects:
TIMESTAMP for temporal grouping
By explicitly casting:
CAST(... AS TIMESTAMP)
the engine spec now produces consistent temporal values, preventing incorrect year rendering and ensuring correct chart grouping.
Fixes #37608

BEFORE/AFTER SCREENSHOTS OR ANIMATED GIF
Not applicable — this change affects SQL generation and backend type handling.

TESTING INSTRUCTIONS
Connect Superset to an Oracle database.
Create a dataset with a datetime column.
Build a chart (e.g., Box Plot or time-series chart).
Apply a time grain such as Month.
Verify:
Dates render correctly.
No incorrect year values appear.

Run unit tests:
pytest tests/unit_tests/db_engine_specs/test_oracle.py


Confirm the updated test passes.

 ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
- [x] Has associated issue: Fixes #37608
- [ ] Required feature flags:
- [ ] Changes UI
- [ ] Includes DB Migration
- [ ] Introduces new feature or API
- [ ] Removes existing feature or API

prs.title: feat: implement Light/Dark theme toggle (fix #161)
prs.body:
## Description
This PR implements a light/dark theme toggle across the SaveBook application, addressing the lack of theme-switching support. The implementation uses next-themes to provide a persistent, accessible, and system-aware theme experience.
Fixes #161
Motivation & Context
Users requested the ability to switch between light and dark themes for better accessibility and usability. This change introduces a consistent theme system integrated into the app layout and navigation while preserving existing design behaviour.


## Type of change
-[x] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)


Changes Made
Installed next-themes to manage theme state and persistence.
Created ThemeProvider:
Client-side provider wrapping the entire application.
Uses class-based theming with system preference support.
Created ThemeToggle:
UI component using Sun and Moon icons from lucide-react.
Accessible toggle button with aria-label.
Updated RootLayout:
Wrapped application with ThemeProvider.
Added suppressHydrationWarning to the <html> tag (required by next-themes).
Updated Navbar:
Integrated Theme Toggle in both desktop and mobile navigation.
Updated background and text classes to support light mode and dark mode.
Updated globals.css:
Applied dynamic background and text colours using the dark class.
Ensured smooth transitions between themes.


## How Has This Been Tested?

Automated Tests
No automated test suite exists in the project.
Ran npm run lint.
Existing lint warnings/errors were already present.
No new warnings or errors were introduced by this PR.
Manual/Browser Testing
Ran the application locally at http://127.0.0.1:3000.
Verification steps:
Located the theme toggle button in the Navbar (aria-label="Toggle Theme").
The verified default theme starts in dark mode.
Toggled to Light mode:
dark class removed from <html>.
Background and text colours updated correctly.
Toggled back to dark mode:
Dark class reapplied.
Refreshed the page:
Confirmed theme preference persists across reloads.


## Checklist:
- [x] My code follows the style guidelines of this project
- [x] I have performed a self-review of my own code
- [x] I have commented my code where necessary
- [x] I have made corresponding changes to the documentation (styles/theme setup)
- [x] My changes generate no new warnings
- [ ] I have added tests that prove my fix is effective (no test framework present)
- [x] New and existing functionality works locally with my changes

prs.title: Fix #124: Improve mobile responsiveness across main pages and components
prs.body:
Summary
This PR fixes mobile responsiveness issues across the Algoryth application to ensure a consistent and usable experience on small and medium screen sizes. The changes remove unintended horizontal scrolling, improve layout stacking on mobile, and make navigation and workspaces adapt correctly to narrow viewports.

Related issues
Fixes #124

Changes Made
Header & Navigation (layout.jsx)
Made navigation links horizontally scrollable on mobile using overflow-x-auto.
Added shrink-0 to prevent nav items from squishing or wrapping.
Reduced horizontal padding on smaller screens (px-6 → px-4).
Home Page Grid (page.jsx)
Updated grid breakpoints so the sidebar stacks below main content on tablets and mobile instead of remaining side-by-side.
Problem Workspace (ProblemWorkspace.jsx)
Replaced non-standard fixed height classes with responsive calc()-based heights.
Improved vertical stacking of problem description and code editor for mobile users.
SplitPane (SplitPane.jsx)
Reduced minPrimary and minSecondary widths from 280/320 to 150px.
Prevented panes from enforcing widths larger than mobile screens, eliminating horizontal overflow.
Footer (Footer.jsx)
Adjusted padding and grid spacing so footer sections stack cleanly on mobile.
Verification Results
Breakpoints tested: 320px, 425px, 768px, 1024px
Horizontal scrolling: Eliminated on all major pages
Usability: Navigation remains easily tappable via horizontal swipe
Layout stability: No overlapping components or broken layouts observed

Checklist
 I ran npm run lint locally
 I ran npm run build locally
 This change is scoped and focused

prs.title: Day 92 Capstone: Hackathon Registration Portal
prs.body:
This PR completes the missing Day 92 Capstone project by adding a fully functional Hackathon Registration Portal, upgrading the day from a static preview to an interactive, scalable application.

What’s Included

Complete project under public/Day 92/

Registration system with input validation and persistent storage

Team creation and join flow (simulated)

Hidden admin dashboard showing all participants

Modern dark UI with glassmorphism and animations

package.json and next.config.js to establish a Next.js foundation

Technical Notes

Uses LocalStorage to simulate a PostgreSQL database, keeping the portal fully functional in a static environment while remaining backend-ready.

Result

Day 92 is now complete, functional, and production-quality as a learning reference.

Closes #508

prs.title: feat: implement login redirection to dashboard and add tech fields to…
prs.body: This PR implements all requested changes from Issue #390, focusing on improving post-login navigation and enhancing project cards with technology stack information. Changes Implemented 1. Login Redirection to Dashboard Renamed website/pages/home.html → website/pages/dashboard.html. Updated authentication and route-guard logic to redirect authenticated users to dashboard.html: website/scripts/auth/service.js website/scripts/auth/guard.js Updated all internal links and navigation labels across HTML files to use Dashboard instead of Home. Verified that home.html no longer exists and no stale references remain. Result: Users are now consistently redirected to the Dashboard after successful authentication. 2. Project Tech Field Addition Added a tech field to all 100 projects in the allProjects array: { day: 1, title: "Personal Portfolio", tech: ["HTML", "CSS", "JS"] } Updated the renderProjects function to dynamically render technology tags on each project card. Added new styles in website/styles/pages/projects.css for tech tags, following a modern glassmorphism aesthetic aligned with the Zenith design system. Implemented hover glow effects for better visual feedback. Result: Each project card now clearly displays its technology stack in a clean, modern UI. Code Highlights Updated Redirection Logic setTimeout(() => { sessionStorage.setItem('authToken', 'true'); window.location.href = 'dashboard.html'; }, 1000); Tech Tags Rendering const techTags = project.tech ? project.tech.map(t => `${t}`).join('') : ''; Verification Confirmed dashboard.html is present and home.html is removed. Verified all relative links in website/pages/*.html point to Dashboard. Visual inspection confirms tech-stack tags render correctly and match the theme. Related Issue Closes #390

prs.title: Add unit test for duplicate REST API token name handling in UserController
prs.body:
Description

This pull request adds a missing unit test to verify proper error handling in UserController::createRestApiToken when a REST API token is created with a duplicate name. The test ensures that a DuplicateTokenNameException is correctly translated into an HTTP 409 Conflict response, as intended by the controller logic.

Changes

Added a new unit test in src/www/ui_tests/api/Controllers/UserControllerTest.php.

Imported required exception classes:

Fossology\UI\Api\Exceptions\HttpConflictException

Fossology\Lib\Exceptions\DuplicateTokenNameException

Implemented testCreateRestApiTokenDuplicateName to:

Mock UserEditPage::generateNewToken to throw DuplicateTokenNameException.

Assert that UserController::createRestApiToken throws HttpConflictException (409).

How to test

Install dependencies using Composer (local installation can be used if Composer is not globally available):

php composer.phar install


Run the UserController unit tests:

./vendor/bin/phpunit src/www/ui_tests/api/Controllers/UserControllerTest.php


Confirm that all tests pass.

Test results

Tests executed: 13

Assertions: 21

Status: PASSED

Note: Tests were executed in a PHP 8.5 environment. Some deprecation warnings were observed due to version differences (FOSSology targets PHP 7.4), but the test logic and results are valid.

Closes #3191
